nameMeanings.name: Amico
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Salvatore, Sal, Lorenzo, Cataldo, Giovanna, Antonio, Arcangelo, Carmine, Cosmo, Dante.

Italian: from a medieval personal name or nickname, Amicus (from Latin amicus ‘friend’), presenting religious connotations, or from a short form of an omen or well-wishing name such as Bonamico (meaning ‘good friend’).

variants.name: Amicucci
meanings: Italian: patronymic or plural form of the personal name Amicuccio, a pet form of Amico .

variants.name: Amici
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Dei, Elio, Enio, Ezio, Ilio, Marino, Nino, Rocco.  French Camille.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of the personal name   Amico  . This surname is also found in southern France.

variants.name: De Amicis
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Aldo, Dante, Eliseo, Franco, Giancarlo, Giulio, Guido, Libero, Lido.

Italian: patronymic from the personal name Amico . It is formed with the Latin ablative plural suffix -is imparting the broader sense ‘belonging to, of’, in this case ‘member of the Amici family’ (compare Amici ).

variants.name: Amicone
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Franco.
Italian: from an augmentative of Amico , probably in the sense of protégé ‘pupil’.

variants.name: D'Amico
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Angelo, Sal, Antonio, Pasquale, Rocco, Carmine, Domenic, Santo, Vito, Carlo, Vincenzo.
Italian: patronymic from the personal name   Amico  .

nameMeanings.name: Di
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Chinese:

Mandarin form of the surname 狄, a term referring to the tribes in ancient northern China: (i) from Di (狄), the name of an ancient state (thought to have been located in Hebei or Shandong province), which was granted to Xiao Bo or Kao Bo by King Cheng of Zhou (reigned 1042–1021  BC ). (ii) adopted as a surname by the Ku Di (庫狄) family during the Northern Wei dynasty (386–534  AD ). (iii) traced back to other minority ethnic groups in ancient northwestern China.

Mandarin form of the Chinese surname 邸, meaning ‘official residence’ in Chinese: (i) from placename Di (邸), the name of a county (unidentified location). (ii) said to be borne by some descendants of Kujula Kadphises (called 丘就郤 or 邸就郤 in Chinese, 30–c. 80 AD ), founder of the Kushan Empire (one branch of the Yuezhi people originating from western China) located in Central Asia during the Eastern Han dynasty (25–220 AD ).

variants.name: Chi
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Dong, Yun, Cheng, Jae, Yong, Cheng Chung, Chi, Chun, Duk, Fang, Feng.  Korean Chung, Chong, Jae Hong, Moon, Myong, Pong, Shen, Yoo, Byung, Chang, Chol, Chong Hun.  Southeast Asian Phi, Hung, Ly, Tu, Denh, Do Van, Duong, Hao, Kieu, Mui, Nga, Phat.

Chinese: Mandarin and Cantonese form of the surname 池, meaning ‘moat’ in ancient Chinese: (i) acquired as a surname by people who lived beside a moat. (ii) from Chi (池), said to be the name of an ancient state (of unknown location and period). (iii) from the personal name Chi (池), personal name of a son of an official in the state of Qin during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ).

Chinese: Mandarin and Cantonese form of the surname 遲, meaning ‘late’ in Chinese: (i) borne by descendants of Chi Ren (遲任), a person who lived during the Shang dynasty (1600–1046  BC ). (ii) shortened form of the Chinese compound surname Yu-Chi (尉遲), originally the name of a tribe from the Xianbei ethnic group in northern China. (iii) from the second element of the personal name Zi Chi (子遲), style name of Fan Xu, a student of Confucius (551–479  BC ).

nameMeanings.name: Grillo
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Rocco, Angelo, Vito, Mario, Sal, Carmela, Orlando, Aldo, Alfredo, Carlo, Domenic, Evelio, Rosario, Pasquale, Antonio, Carmine, Raul, Roberto, Sergio.
Italian: nickname for a cheerful person, from grillo ‘cricket’ (from Late Latin grillus; see Grill 1). This surname is also found in southern France.

variants.name: Grill
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Franz, Otto, Alois, Bernhard, Ernst, Gerhard, Hermann, Klaus, Konrad, Manfred, Monika.

German: nickname for a cheerful person, from Middle High German grille ‘cricket’ (Old High German grillo, from Late Latin grillus, Greek gryllos). The insect is widely supposed to be of a cheerful disposition, no doubt because of its habit of preferring hearths and warm places. The vocabulary word is confined largely to southern Germany and Austria, and it is in this region that the surname is most frequent. This surname is also found in Czechia and Slovenia (see 4 below).
German: habitational name from any of the eight places in Upper Bavaria and Austria, perhaps so named from Middle High German grille ‘cricket’ (see 1 above).

variants.name: Grilli
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Guido, Salvatore, Donato, Enzo, Vito, Aldo, Angelo, Dino, Enrico, Ettore, Francesco, Geno.
Italian:
patronymic or plural form of   Grillo  .

nameMeanings.name: Giovanni
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From Latin. See John .

variants.name: John
meanings: As a boy's name this represents a revival of Middle English Jan, a by‐form of John , or an adoption of the common European form with this spelling. As a girl's name it is a short form of names such as Janet and Janice .

en.lifeSummary: Don Giovanni Amico Di Grillo was born about 1157, in Grillo, Nocera Umbra, Perugia, Umbria, Italy. He had at least 1 daughter with Donna Anna Amico Di Grillo. He died in Genova, Liguria, Italy.
